title: State Theatre events from July 20 through July 26
author: Jackson Malenke | The Daily Collegian
description:
The State Theatre is continuing the summer with more movies, music and events.

Here’s what to expect this week.

Wednesday, July 22

“The Bad Guys 2”

The State Theatre is collaborating with Schlow Library to bring children’s movies to the screen.

This week, they’re showing a beloved sequel, “The Bad Guys 2,” where the – now reformed – group of bad guys are trying to be good. Unexpectedly, The Bad Girls join the story.

The screening is at noon in the Friedman Auditorium, and it’s free with registration.

“Escape From New York”

The State Theatre is taking it back to 1981 with “Escape From New York.” In this movie, a mercenary must save the president from a prison island of murderers, terrorists and more before time runs out.

The film is at 7:30 p.m. in the Friedman Auditorium. Tickets are $12.68 for adults and $10.68 for students, sold online or at the door.

Thursday, July 23

“Summer Tour”

A newer movie from 2025, “Summer Tour” follows a couple of devout fans during the final tour of Dead and Company as they journey across America.

The documentary is at 7:30 p.m. in the Friedman Auditorium for $12.68 or $10.58 for students, sold online or at the door.

Friday, July 24

“The Commitments”

This movie follows a Dublin Soul band, The Commitments, through their rise and fall.

The showing is at 8 p.m. in the Friedman Auditorium for $12.68 or $10.58 for students, sold online or at the door.

Saturday, July 25

“Alien”

The State Theatre will begin Saturday with the horror classic “Alien.”

This movie follows the crew of a spaceship introduced to an unknown lifeform. The crew must prevent it from getting to Earth.

The screening is at 8 p.m. in the Friedman Auditorium for $12.68 or $10.58 for students, sold online or at the door.

The Price Sisters

At the same time, The Price Sisters will be playing live in The Attic.

The Price Sisters are twins who have grown up around music. The sisters have now been performing together at various venues for over 7 years.

The event is at 8 p.m. for $30.08. Tickets are on sale now.

Sunday, July 26

The Lone Bellow

The live music continues with The Lone Bellow’s What a Time to be Alive Tour, promoting the band’s newest album of the same name.

The Lone Bellow has topped American song charts, while also being on The Tonight Show and The Late Show. The band said it’s committed to making music and building its community.

The show is at 8 p.m. in the Friedman Auditorium for $44.92. Tickets are on sale now.

title: Penn State Board of Trustees raise tuition; approve 15% performance bonus for Bendapudi
author: Saxtyn Lutz | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Penn State Board of Trustees approved tuition, room and board increases for undergraduate students at the University Park campus for the 2027-28 academic year at a meeting on Friday.

In-state undergraduate students at University Park will have a tuition increase of 2.5%. Out-of-state undergraduate students will have a 4% increase in tuition. There will be increased cost for housing and food across all campuses.

There will be no tuition increase for Pennsylvania resident undergraduate students at Penn State’s Commonwealth campuses. Out-of-state undergraduate students at Commonwealth campuses will have a 1%.

The board also approved a 15% presidential performance bonus for President Neeli Bendapudi to her $1.4 million base salary, which made her the second highest paid president of a public university in the United States when it was approved in September 2025.

Bendapudi, appointed in 2022, has the third longest tenure of the current Big Ten presidents or chancellors, next to the presidents of University of Maryland (six years) and University of Iowa (five years). The average turnover rate for presidents is a little over 1.5 years, according to Paterno.

“I think it's important that we understand the marketplace that we are in and why these things are meaningful and important to do,” Paterno said to the board.

A budget of $11.1 billion was approved for the fiscal year beginning July 2027. This budget covers tuition, fees, housing and food rates.

MORE NEWS COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's volleyball to face Stanford at the Palestra in Philadelphia
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is heading to the City of Brotherly Love this upcoming season.

The Nittany Lions will play Stanford at the Palestra in Philadelphia on Sept. 11. Penn State's schedule on its website lists this match as a neutral-site contest, and a first-serve time is still to be announced.

The Palestra opened in 1927 and is the home to the University of Pennsylvania's basketball programs. Charles Klauder was the architect for the Palestra, and he also designed Penn State's home gym, Rec Hall.

Known as the Cathedral of College Basketball, the Palestra has hosted recent Penn State men's basketball games as well. This upcoming match will be the first time in 41 years that the women's volleyball team has played in the venue.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse earns commitment from attacker Reese Heim
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a busy start to the offseason in the Transfer Portal, coach Kayla Treanor has added a major high school prospect.

Class of 2026 attacker Reese Heim announced her pledge to join the Nittany Lions on Instagram after a two-year varsity career at West Chester Henderson High School and club tenure with NXT LC Volt.

The righty stands at 5-foot-7, and brings an accomplished career to Happy Valley for her next four years of eligibility. Heim earned praise as a USA Lacrosse Player of the Week this past June after a performance in which she netted seven goals in an effort to seize the Pennsylvania District 1 AAA championship, her school’s first since 1998. Overall, she scored 71 goals, second for Henderson last year.

Heim was originally committed to join Marquette in 2024, before eventually committing to join Penn State next season.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball to host Canisius for 2026-27 season opener
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
The countdown until Penn State returns to action can begin.

The Nittany Lions will host Canisius on Nov. 2, according to a report by Sam Federman. With the game happening on the Monday of the first week of the college basketball season, this will seemingly be Penn State's season opener.

The Nittany Lions hold a 4-0 advantage over the Golden Griffins in their all-time series. The two teams most recently met in the 2022-23 season, when Penn State earned a 97-67 win.

Penn State hasn't announced its full non-conference schedule yet, but it did announce a game against Pitt at the Palestra in Philadelphia on Nov. 8. The Nittany Lions will also be participating in the 2026 Battle 4 Atlantis during Thanksgiving week.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: People, names every student should know as they step onto campus for the first time
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
Stepping onto Penn State’s campus for the first time as a student can be overwhelming, on top of that the incoming class often hears random names as though they should know them.

Here are names that get thrown around a lot that every new student should know.

President Neeli Bendapudi

The 19th president of Penn State University has been in office since July 1, 2022 semester. Bendapudi is both the first woman and first person of color to hold the role at Penn State.

The Willard Preacher

Outside no matter the weather stands a man in a red hoodie, or if it’s exceptionally cold, a dark colored puffer, quoting the Bible to passersby.

The real name of this Willard Building staple is Gary Cattell, but he is more often than not referred to as The Willard Preacher.

UPUA President Kat Hogan

The University Park Undergraduate Association (UPUA) is Penn State’s student government.

President Kat Hogan was elected on April 1 to lead the 21st assembly, which spans the majority of the 2026-27 school year.

UPUA Vice President Maria Lopez

Elected alongside Hogan on April 1, Lopez is currently serving as the Vice President of UPUA.

Hogan and Lopez ran their campaign on the goals of increasing student engagement with UPUA and transparency across the university.

Matt Campbell

Entering his first season as the head football coach of the Nittany Lions, Matt Campbell previously coached at Iowa State for a decade.

All eyes will be on Campbell this season after a disappointing 2025 season under former coach James Franklin.

Be sure to try to spot him on the field during this season’s games.

Mayor Ezra Nanes

Ezra Nanes, Mayor of the Borough of State College, can be seen at events across town and campus as he seeks to build a relationship between the borough, the university and the students living here.

Nanes himself is a Penn State alum after graduating with a master’s in marketing and finance in 2012.

State College Police Chief Joseph Merill

Merill was sworn into office in January and became the ninth police chief to serve the borough of State College.

The State College native served as police chief for Altoona for four years.

University Police and Public Safety (UPPS)

UPPS is Penn State’s own police department that patrols campus and surrounding areas. UPPS works in tandem with the university and local partners to ensure the safety of students.

Your Academic Advisor

Unlike high school counselors, academic advisors are well-versed in the world that surrounds your particular major.

Semesterly or yearly meetings can ensure you, as a student, are able to accomplish all the academic goals you have for yourself at Penn State.

title: Guide from an out-of-stater turned Penn Stater
author: Alli Satterfield | News Editor
description:
Transitioning to college is daunting to begin with, and when you choose a school far away from home, that change can be even more difficult.

Being an out-of-state student can mean a lot of things. You might have different perspectives, expectations or norms than your peers, but that also means you can share insights they may not have.

I grew up in South Carolina, and the cultural norms from the Southeastern coast to Central Pennsylvania are a bit different.

Here are some of the things I learned to make your transition slightly easier. Think of it as an out-of-state to Penn State guide.

1. You’re somewhere… in the middle of nowhere

When driving into Penn State’s University Park campus, you may notice there’s not a lot going on. The sights and sounds of mooing cows can be a little intimidating for students from more metropolitan areas.

But, never fear, because there are definitely things to do and see on campus and in State College. Our little city in the middle of nowhere also has the perk of being in the middle of nowhere.

While that may sound like a non sequitur, the location means there are plenty of state parks and places for outdoor recreation nearby. It’s kind of the best of both worlds.

2. Pack your whole closet

In South Carolina, I was faced with constant reminders that “it’ll be cold up there” before I came to Penn State. What I didn’t realize is that it would be below freezing, incredibly windy and blisteringly warm all within the same day at Penn State.

The weather here can be a little bit unpredictable, and in my freshman year, I didn’t pack generously enough to allow for that. Frankly, you may need your big winter coat one morning, but be wearing shorts and a t-shirt by the same afternoon.

3. Learn the words to the alma mater (or don’t)

During my first football game, when everyone started singing the alma mater, I was so lost.

Here’s a heads up: They have the lyrics on the jumbotron at Beaver Stadium. You don’t actually have to know the lyrics, which is a great cheat code for students who maybe didn’t have the Penn State upbringing that some in-state students did.

In the Bryce Jordan Center, the lyrics are written around the arena on the ribbon board, so no matter where you are, there’s probably a fail-safe if you forget the lyrics.

So learn the lyrics — or don’t. It’s up to you.

4. Take the whoop!

I refused to learn the bus system at Penn State my freshman year. I lived in South Halls, and I said to myself, “I must walk everywhere.” This year, I’m a resident assistant living in East Halls, and my building is just so far from all of my classes.

Luckily, the CATA bus stop is right in front of my dorm building, and, even though I didn’t know how to navigate the bus system at first, it has been the biggest blessing. The White Loop (whoop) goes right from my doorstep across campus and downtown, which I love.

And it’s free, so I highly recommend taking advantage of the bus system sooner than I did.

5. Invest in a dolly

Since I just recommended basically bringing your whole wardrobe to school with you, I have to concede that the process of moving does involve a lot of active parts. I’ve found it’s easier to use reusable tote boxes to move, but the really handy tool is a dolly.

It’s easy to load three to five boxes at a time, and that means fewer trips in and out of your room on move-in day. You can thank me later.

I wish all of our new out-of-state students the best. It takes everyone to make us who “We Are.” (Penn State!)

title: Column | Where does Matt Campbell rank among the best coaches in college football?
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Matt Campbell has proven himself as a winner no matter where he goes. After a stint at Toledo where he won two bowl games, he turned Iowa State into a team that was battling for the Big 12 Championship Game throughout his time in Ames.

From a holistic perspective of college football, there are some new teams in contention and coaches taking over different programs, resulting in the college football landscape drastically changing over the past couple of years.

Campbell is the latest to take the next jump, this time into the Big Ten and a major brand at Penn State. It now begs the question: Where does Campbell rank among the best coaches in college football?

1. Curt Cignetti, Indiana

Curt Cignetti is coming off winning Indiana’s first national championship and has completely transformed a program that seemed almost impossible to make a top team in the nation.

Everywhere Cignetti goes, he wins, and he’s proven that already by turning Indiana into a national program. The major question is now if Cignetti can keep Indiana near or at the top of the mountain for multiple years down the line.

2. Ryan Day, Ohio State

Ryan Day always has Ohio State at the top of the rankings and winning at the highest level. The 2024 national champions finished 2025 with a 12-2 record, losing its final two games to Indiana in the Big Ten championship and Miami in the College Football Playoff.

There was no drop off in success when Day took over for Urban Meyer, and he has the Buckeyes set up for success in the future. Day will have his biggest test in 2026 as Ohio State has to travel to Texas, Indiana, Iowa and USC.

3. Kirby Smart, Georgia

The only other head coach with a national title and recent winning success, Kirby Smart has made Georgia one of the most consistent teams in the country. Smart led the Bulldogs to a 12-2 record in 2025, and they’re consistently in the College Football Playoff.

4. Dan Lanning, Oregon

Dan Lanning has had the most coaching success in the country without winning a national title. Oregon is coming off a College Football Playoff semifinal loss to Indiana and has made the 12-team bracket both years in its existence.

The Ducks are one of the favorites entering the year to win it all and with a returning Dante Moore, they could do just that with Lanning at the helm.

5. Mario Cristobal, Miami

Mario Cristobal is as high as he is because of recent success with Miami, which finished as the national runner ups last year. But he hasn’t always had his team at this level, as he couldn’t succeed at Oregon.

Cristobal has figured things out at Miami, though, and with the new era of the transfer portal and recent quarterback recruiting, Cristobal has the Hurricanes set for years to come at the most important position.

6. Steve Sarkisian, Texas

Steve Sarkisian has Texas officially back into national title conversations. While last season was a bit of an underwhelming one for the Longhorns — having just missed out on the College Football Playoff — the semifinal appearance the year before shows that Sarkisian can get them there.

With a returning Arch Manning, and added talent in the portal, Sarkisian has the chance to get Texas back into playing deep into December.

7. Marcus Freeman, Notre Dame

Another team that had a “down year” last season, Notre Dame finished 10-2 on the season and just missed out on the College Football Playoff. Marcus Freeman has the Fighting Irish as a consistent top team in the country, even with the team not being in a conference.

Freeman’s mix of recruiting and coaching makes him one of the top coaches in the country, and a national championship appearance at the end of the 2024 season shows he can win big games.

8. Lane Kiffin, LSU

Lane Kiffin remained in the SEC, but moved to LSU during the offseason. With the success the Tigers have had under other coaches, they could be right back into contention.

Kiffin had Ole Miss at its peak last year, winning big games and making it to the College Football Playoff. However, those wins in the tournament technically don’t count for Kiffin given he was already in Baton Rouge.

With his familiarity with the transfer portal and high-octane offense, Kiffin remains one of the top coaches in the country even with the move to LSU.

9. Mike Elko, Texas A&M

Mike Elko has taken a sudden rise and is the prototype of what big programs want out of coaches coming from “lesser teams.” Elko made his way from Duke after winning with the Blue Devils and has Texas A&M in a great spot.

The Aggies lost to the title runner ups last year, Miami, in the first round of the College Football Playoff, and they’re likely to be around the same spot this year. The future is bright for Elko who holds the consensus top recruiting class in 2027.

10. James Franklin, Virginia Tech

With recency bias, Franklin would be significantly lower overall, but his resume is among the best in the nation.

Franklin slots in at No. 9 at his new job at Virginia Tech, while the biggest question with Franklin at Penn State comes from his track record in the big games. He’ll still face top teams in the nation, but the depth of the schedule isn’t nearly as good now in the ACC.

With a fresh look, Franklin could quickly have the Hokies as a top team in the conference.

11. Kyle Whittingham, Michigan

Kyle Whittingham is entering his first year at Michigan, and his track record at Utah has him inside the top 10. Whittingham went 177-88 at Utah in 22 seasons, which included back-to-back Rose Bowl appearances. He’s had the Utes inside the top 10 of the AP Poll multiple times, and the only question is whether he can have success somewhere else after spending his entire head coaching career with Utah.

12. Matt Campbell, Penn State

Now enters Penn State’s new coach. Campbell never coached in major games at Iowa State, but he still had loads of success with the Cyclones with a 72-55 record and consistent winning seasons.

Campbell has less questions about success in a new location than Whittingham, but hasn’t reached the heights that Whittingham has at their previous jobs. Campbell is coming off an 8-4 season, but had key players injured, and with a 2024 record of 11-3, he’s proven to win a lot during a season.

With a relatively easy schedule for a Big Ten team, Campbell could have the Nittany Lions right back into College Football Playoff contention in 2026.

title: Off the record | Raise the flags
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Red flags. We’ve all heard of them, but how often are we actually listening to them?

I’m guilty of pretending I didn’t hear that off-putting piece of information, and then watching my decision immediately backfire. It happens to the best of us.

Over the years, I have compiled a list of red flags that don’t seem like the end of the world at first, but will have you thinking “why’d I let them do that?” months later. Now, I’m sharing it with all of you.

Can’t converse

Right off the bat, if a partner can’t hold a conversation, especially in the early stages, how are they going to sustain it long-term? Small talk is awkward for everyone, but you aren’t going to get to the deep stuff without it.

When you ask a question, they should be asking one back. There shouldn’t be an eerie silence or blank stares. I think talking to a wall would be easier.

Bad jokes

I don’t mean differing humors, I mean making jokes that are direct digs at your character and interests. Using sarcasm to undermine you isn’t a type of humor, and certainly shouldn’t be a characteristic of someone you want to be in a relationship with. If it hurts your feelings and they show no remorse or improvement, lose their number.

I feel like I shouldn’t even have to say it, but jokes about hurting you or themselves are never funny.

Rude to employees

This is a classic but true nonetheless. If they treat innocent strangers who are just doing their job like crap, why would they treat you any better?

Saying "please" and "thank you" are nonnegotiables, not suggestions.

Family and friends aren’t interested

Another classic, but if your loved ones think something is off, it probably is. They know you the best, so if they aren’t vibing with your relationship, something is afoot.

I understand you might be defensive, but they’re seeing things you aren’t. Trust me, they don’t want to hurt your feelings, even if it only stings a little.

Jealousy

I’m not just talking about them being jealous of other people flirting or talking with you, but rather them being jealous of you. They try to shut down your ambition and drive because they don’t have any, or try to persuade you to do things you know you don’t want to do.

At no point should they put you down as a way to make themselves look or even feel better. Jealousy isn’t a flattering color on anyone, let alone someone you’re hoping is your “soulmate.”

Saying “I love you” too quickly

I have no experience with this, but I have a friend whose boyfriend said the iconic three words within a week of knowing each other. There’s no possible way that they truly mean it (sorry!), and it feels like a way to keep you in a relationship you both know isn’t working.

It’s harder to leave someone who’s just confessed their undying love for you. Don’t fall into the trap.

Being a mama’s boy

Being family-oriented is one thing, but we all know a mama’s boy who can do wrong in the eyes of his mother. When he’s never had to take accountability, there’s no way he’s going to start now.

And a lack of accountability is just one thing. What’s more is he’s going to expect you to do all the things his mom did for him — even the little things anyone past the age of 13 should be able to do themselves, like do their own laundry. Weaponized competence is a real thing, and don’t you forget it!

You’ll never be good enough for her precious son, so don’t even try.

Talks badly about their exes

If they’re talking badly about every single one of their exes, the enemy is closer than we think. The common denominator is them; there’s no way all of the people they’ve dated are as evil and problematic as they’re describing.

I can’t help but think the “crazy” behavior you’ve been told about is a direct reaction to the way they, instead, were treated by your current partner.

No real friends

If all of their friends are their ex-talking stages or situationships, I need you to leave ASAP.

Maybe it’s kind of controversial, but if they can’t keep a solid friend group, they’re no good. Our friends are a good indicator of the type of person we are, so if they don’t have any, time to wake up.

In your dating journey, you're bound to encounter the reddest of flags. It’s crucial you don’t ignore them and instead run for the hills.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

title: Neeli Bendapudi, Pat Kraft issue letter to Sen. John Fetterman on concerns of Protect College Sports Act
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
On June 17, Penn State president Neeli Bendapudi sent a letter to Pennsylvania Sen. John Fetterman with concerns regarding the Protect College Sports Act.

Bendapudi also joined the presidents of Ohio State, USC and Michigan in a visit to Sens. Maria Cantwell and Ted Cruz on Tuesday.

Bendapudi and Vice President for Intercollegiate Athletics Pat Kraft were hopeful that the revised bill on June 11 would address many issues they, and the Big Ten saw in the initial bill.

However, only one of those issues were fully addressed, relating to institutional NIL agreements. Following the opening paragraph, Bendapudi listed six different topics that they felt hindered not only Penn State, but also the Big Ten and SEC.

A majority of the points listed deal with NIL and how money is handled between athletes, collectives and the universities. Bendapudi’s first point breaks down that there wasn’t any adjustment to the wording on NIL deals and a cap, essentially denying student athletes from high-revenue generating sports like men’s basketball and football millions of dollars in earnings.

They later pointed out the lack of adjustment in wording with athletes using the arbitration process to earn money, which was put forth through House settlement that allows for revenue sharing. The process would allow athletes to challenge earnings, similar to the common process in the NHL and MLB, where players can challenge in court for higher earnings.

Bendapudi’s other major point related to roster spots and eligibility. A new section was added that would lock in scholarship and roster spots in non-football and basketball sports as is from the 2024-25 roster limits. Bendapudi feels that has a negative impact on institutions in the Big Ten who’ve already provided major opportunities and support for women’s and Olympic sports.

They mention the limits and narrowness of the bill in relation to legal actions. Bendapudi and Kraft feel that the protection remains so limited that it won’t put a stop to lawsuits at the state level getting involved with college sports.

Also, they mention the College Sports Commission, or CSC, which was added from the House settlement, was supposed to act alone, rather than with the NCAA. Ultimately, it’s providing less protection for universities in these lawsuits.

Bendapudi and Kraft close out their letter to Fetterman with little substantial changes to Title II, which severely affects the Big Ten and SEC and no other conferences. They continue to remain committed to engagement to improve the legislation as it continues to go through the legal system.

title: Former Penn State RB Tikey Hayes to join Nebraska
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion is joining Matt Rhule's squad in Lincoln, Nebraska.

Running back Tikey Hayes is joining Nebraska, he confirmed via his Instagram bio.

Hayes joined Penn State following an illustrious high school career at Aliquippa in the WPIAL. The former 4-star recruit didn't see much time during his first season with the Nittany Lions, but made an impact in Penn State's Pinstripe Bowl victory over Clemson.

Following the season, Hayes announced he'd return to Penn State on Jan. 7, but four days later entered the transfer portal. Despite still having connections with the coaching staff as the nephew of Terry Smith, the running back chose to explore elsewhere.

Hayes' next destination remained unknown for multiple months before landing at Iowa Western Community College. After working out with the Reivers in the spring, Hayes will now join Nebraska, holding four years of eligibility and a chance at more playing time in the Big Ten.

If he remains with the Cornhuskers after the 2026 season, he'll be joined by former Penn State wide receiver commit Khalil Taylor as Rhule continues to go after players from western Pennsylvania.

title: 5 key targets for Penn State in the 2028 recruiting class
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
With the Class of 2027 recruiting efforts starting to wind down, Matt Campbell is turning his focus to the following year’s crop of high school prospects.

The 2028 class will be his first year with a fully fledged recruiting period, having settled into his role as Penn State’s head coach. Given the time to build relationships in Pennsylvania and the surrounding regions, it’ll be interesting to see his recruiting growth going forward.

That said, here are five key targets for Penn State’s 2028 class recruiting efforts.

EDGE - George Parkinson IV

Matt Campbell’s lack of success with recruiting in Pennsylvania wasn’t a big shock, considering he’s a new face in the Keystone State for the 2027 class. However, the Class of 2028 presents new opportunities to lock down the homefront.

4-star edge George Parkinson IV is a crucial in-state target, ranking as the No. 2 player out of Pennsylvania and No. 14 edge in the 2028 class.

Parkinson recently announced on social media that he’d shaved down his list of schools to six, with Penn State making the cut alongside Ohio State, Oregon, Texas A&M, Tennessee and LSU. He noted that these six programs have made him “feel like a priority.”

He’s shown particular interest in the Nittany Lions and Volunteers. Penn State offered the 6-foot-5, 235-pound defender on Jan. 24, the same day he attended Junior Day. Parkinson later took an unofficial visit to Knoxville in March after being offered the month prior.

Securing Parkinson would be the best way for Campbell to better establish a foothold in Pennsylvania while also adding an elite talent to Christian Smith’s defensive end room.

EDGE - Jermaine Smith Jr.

Another blue chip edge prospect to look out for is Jermaine Smith Jr. In his sophomore season, he helped St. Frances Academy achieve a 9-1 overall record and defeat Utah’s Corner Canyon High School, 37-20, in the inaugural Overtime national championship game.

Hailing from Baltimore, he’s the No. 2 player out of Maryland and the No. 50 player in the 2028 class, per 247Sports composite. If he chooses the Nittany Lions, Smith would be Campbell’s first top 50 recruit at Penn State, barring any prior commitments.

Smith has also continuously shown interest in Penn State. While he’s not an in-state recruit, he’s been on two total unofficial visits, and both were to Happy Valley. His first visit was on March 28, during which he was offered, and he later returned on June 20.

So far, Campbell has struggled to recruit in Washington D.C, Maryland and Virginia, locations where James Franklin made his bread and butter. A landmark recruit like Smith may be the spark that attracts more attention to Penn State from the D.C. area.

In addition, pairing Smith with Parkinson — if they both choose the Nittany Lions — would formulate one of the strongest young edge rooms in the nation.

However, the 6-foot-5, 215-pound edge rusher has also received an offer from Ohio State, which he pinned to the top of his X page.

WR - David Forster

Penn State already lost one wide receiver battle to Nebraska in Khalil Taylor, but there’s a redemption opportunity in the Class of 2028 with David Forster, who is also being actively recruited by the Cornhuskers.

The 3-star out of Montvale, New Jersey, was originally offered by James Franklin’s staff in May 2025. He was re-offered by the new staff after a spring practice visit on April 18, the same day that St. Joseph Regional High School teammate Jonathon Galette committed to the Nittany Lions.

Additionally, current running backs coach Savon Huggins and director of recruiting Naz Oliver originate from New Jersey. Forster labeled the pair his “hometown heroes” on social media prior to his April visit.

Campbell has found success recruiting from New Jersey, with five prospects already committed to the Nittany Lions in the 2027 class, the most out of any state.

At The Opening’s New Jersey Regional combine in April, Forster ran a 4.46-second 40-yard dash and a 4.28-second shuttle, while his vertical jump came in at 45.4 inches. The 6-foot-4.5, 212-pounder was then placed on the Northeast roster for The Opening Finals, an all-star camp in Eugene, Oregon, that was held on June 24.

RB - Tahmere Brown

Also from New Jersey, 4-star Tahmere Brown could fill the hole left behind by Aiden Gibson, who recently flipped from Penn State to Rutgers.

Chukwuma Odoh is the only running back still committed to Penn State, and he is also from New Jersey. The timing of Brown’s arrival may prove pivotal to an early playing role if he chooses Penn State and challenges Odoh in the depth chart.

Brown ranks as the No. 8 player out of New Jersey and the No. 12 running back in the Class of 2028. The 5-foot-11, 188-pound running back currently plays for The Pennington School and also plays 7-on-7 with Pittsburgh-based AD 2Tenths alongside current Penn State commit Dhillon McGee in the OT7 spring league.

Following the end of his sophomore season, Brown played in the 2026 Navy All-American Bowl on Jan. 9 in San Antonio, Texas.

LB - Brandon Murphy Jr.

Campbell can keep up the 2028 in-state recruiting momentum by securing the next addition to “Linebacker U” with Clairton native Brandon Murphy Jr.

A 4-star prospect, Murphy was offered in May 2025 and has kept Penn State top of mind, routinely returning to Happy Valley under Terry Smith’s guidance. He attended Junior Day in January before returning to visit the Nittany Lions on March 24.

Murphy ranks as the No. 6 player out of Pennsylvania and the No. 14 linebacker in the Class of 2028. He’s an all-around athlete, also playing wide receiver, safety and cornerback for Clairton High School as a sophomore.

In 2025, he recorded 121 tackles, 23 tackles for loss and four sacks while forcing five fumbles. In the defensive backfield, Murphy broke up five passes and caught three interceptions on the year through the air.

The year prior, he was named a first-team Freshman All-American by MaxPreps after making 102 tackles and 23 tackles for loss.

title: Potential trap games for Penn State in 2026
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Compared to recent years, Penn State’s 2026 schedule is on the easier end. It doesn’t face reigning national champion Indiana or annual contenders Ohio State and Oregon.

But the Nittany Lions do face Michigan, Washington and USC, all of which are projected to finish in the top half of the Big Ten. Outside of those three opponents, Penn State’s schedule is relatively weak with a handful of typically mediocre teams on the slate.

Upsets can happen, though, so here’s a look at some potential trap games for Penn State.

Oct. 2 vs Northwestern

Northwestern rewrote Penn State history last season when it beat the Nittany Lions 22-21, which ended up being the last straw of James Franklin’s 12-year tenure, as Franklin was fired the following day.

The Wildcats will look to extend that winning streak against the Nittany Lions to two when they debut their brand new stadium, Ryan Field, on Oct. 2.

The new stadium will be the smallest venue in the Big Ten with a capacity of just 35,000. However, the student section is designed after European soccer venues, as it’s built at a 33-degree angle — much steeper than a typical stadium — which may create a tough environment attempting to score in the end zone.

Penn State will have to deal with that during its first conference road game of the year, and consecutive marquee matchups follow against USC and Michigan. With those games looming, it would be easy for the Nittany Lions to overlook Northwestern, setting up a potential trap game.

Nov. 21 vs Rutgers

The Nittany Lions have dominated Rutgers over their 37-year-old rivalry, winning 33 of the 35 matchups between the two teams. However, last year’s game marked one of the closest contests since the Scarlet Knights last won in 1988.

This year’s game will be played in Beaver Stadium in the second-to-last week of the regular season. While Penn State has had success against Rutgers over the years, late-November conference games can become dangerous with fatigue, injuries and postseason implications.

In 2025, it was a high-scoring affair between both sides, including a high-yardage battle in the trenches. Kaytron Allen led the way with 226 yards, while the Scarlet Knights ran for 195 yards with 338 passing yards.

This year, Penn State will once again have to deal with star wide receiver K.J. Duff and running back Antwan Raymond, so it’s possible Rutgers replicates its offensive success and catches the Nittany Lions off guard.

Nov. 14 vs Minnesota

Despite being a pair of Big Ten rivals who square off for the Governor's Victory Bell trophy, Penn State and Minnesota have faced each other just four times in the past 10 years. This season, the Nittany Lions will host the Golden Gophers in Beaver Stadium for the first time since 2022.

Three of the squads’ four most recent games have been decided by a single score, including a one-point Penn State victory in 2024, an overtime win by Penn State in 2016 and a five-point Minnesota triumph in 2019.

While Penn State will be coming off a cross-country trip to Washington — one of its more difficult opponents in 2026 — the Golden Gophers will have hosted UCLA, an easier Big Ten foe.

If the Nittany Lions fall to the Huskies, a losing streak could commence if they don’t prepare themselves for Minnesota. On the flip side, if Penn State won, a hangover is always a possibility.

The last few contests have featured big moments from each side — Saquon Barkley ended the 2016 matchup with a 25-yard rushing touchdown in overtime, while Luke Reynolds converted a fake punt to help seal Penn State’s win in 2024.

This year’s matchup may not be as dramatic as the past, but could be if Minnesota gets in a groove. Plus, the Golden Gophers always have the potential to play spoiler under P.J. Fleck.

title: Lifelong artist Holly Sue Foss brings color, inspiration to Arts Fest
author: Justin Elliott | The Daily Collegian
description:
Art has been a focal point of 73-year-old Holly Sue Foss' life since she was 5-years-old taking classes with the support of her mother.

She has been attending art shows for 42 years and first began frequenting the Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts in the 90s. The Pittsburgh native relocated from Pennsylvania to Maryland before settling in Minneapolis, Minnesota in 1998 after meeting her late partner, John, who was also an artist, but Foss doesn’t let this 17-hour drive deter her.

“I still come because I love this show. I have a history with the show. So I just keep coming, and I know many of the owners. We have friendships,” Foss said.

Foss’ specialty is serigraphs and acrylic paintings that colorfully depict animals. The process is extensive and done completely by hand, involving drawing, ink, erasers, multiple copies, coloring, peeling and squeegees.

“I’ve done it with kids, I’ve done it with 3-year-olds. It’s not complicated, but it is fun,” she said.

Foss got her basic silkscreen printing training at Carnegie Mellon University before getting a full-ride scholarship to The San Francisco Art Institute, where her style shifted from extinct animals and nature to more colorful ideas.

Between “gray Pittsburgh” and the “pink and blue and cherry” essence of San Francisco, she said the two locations couldn’t have been more opposite.

Hannah Baschour, a 28-year-old sales associate from Pittsburgh, was drawn to the art’s vibrance.

“I really like the color palette,” Baschour said.

While her process hasn’t changed much, Foss most recently shifted from animals to aliens, calling the concept “the new Noah’s Ark.”

She explained the environment-focused thought process behind it, and said the aliens would “gather [the animals] up, and then we clean up the earth, and then they’re gonna bring them back. Their mission is just to take care, because we’re not doing a great job.”

Emily Ibarra, a third-year studying architecture, said Foss’ art tent caught her attention because of the colors and fun shapes.

“It really moves your eye around the page,” Ibarra said.

While she doesn’t have a favorite work of her own in particular, Foss said she was partial to her mandala art. The geometric patterns and repetitive symbols stand out from the rest of her art, which Foss said represents her spiritual side.

“It’s not for everyone, but I still do them, and then occasionally someone really connects to it, and it’s wonderful,” she said.

Foss said that she and John attended Arts Fest together for many years. She recently did a memorial show in his honor at her Minneapolis home, presenting his artwork to fellow artists and friends.

“I feel like losing John, you keep learning, even from that experience. I learned so much and had to grow so much,” she said.

Foss described her art as uplifting, colorful, happy and fun. Through the ups and downs of life, Foss continues learning. She has continued to get new ideas as life goes on.

“Make sure you keep getting inspiration from wherever it comes because it’s a mystery. But when you get it, you have to listen to it,” she said.

title: 'Amazing as always' | Arts Fest 2026 finishes after drawing thousands to State College
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
After five days of art filling the streets of downtown State College, the 2026 Central Pennsylvania Festival of the Arts has come to a close. This summer marked the 60th anniversary of the iconic event, bringing thousands of visitors to State College.

Visitors and artists alike expressed their enjoyment at this year’s festival, from seeing friends, looking at beautiful artwork, and experiencing the State College summer atmosphere.

Brynn Wright, a second-year studying aerospace engineering, is from the State College area and has been attending Arts Fest every year since she was a child.

“It's really cool to see all the art and everything that these people spend so much time making … I always like to go with my friends and get some of the food and listen to the music and stuff,” Wright said. “I think it's one of the best times of the year to be in State College.”

Similarly Kathleen Hume has been attending Arts Fest for many years. As the immediate past president of the Penn State Alumni Association’s Centre County Chapter she’s helped organize events for visiting alumni. Hume said nearly 100,000 alumni visited this summer.

“I think it's just always amazing to walk around and just see people enjoying the music and just really coming back to town and reconnecting with the university in different ways,” Hume said. “We have these maps of the U.S. Pennsylvania, and you can see that people come from all over. We have people from as far away as Japan and Brazil that have come back. So it's really interesting to hear their stories and how they want to come back and reconnect.”

Artist Rita Lohr and her husband have been selling their paintings and mixed-media artwork at Arts Fest for three years. Hailing from Kansas, Lohr said State College’s small-town atmosphere was one of the reasons drawing her back to the festival each year.

“This is probably turning into one of our favorite shows probably because we like the smaller town feel. We also do shows, like, in major cities, like Denver and Milwaukee and those kinds of things. We like more of the vibe you have here,” Lohr said.

Lohr also noted the “good sales” and “wonderful conditions” of the festival, even with the week’s heavy thunderstorms impacting some of the booths.

Greg Stones, a visual artist, said this year’s festival was “amazing as always”, while the weather was “crazy as always.”

Stone has been selling at Arts Fest for 15 years and shared the impact of the community on his experience at the event.

“I've definitely built a big following for one of these years,” Stones said. “It's just a great crowd. People are so enthusiastic about the art. I mean, they're gonna have fun. I always love it.”

Painter Alex Ramos also mentioned the community at Arts Fest, specifically the artist community he has become familiar with during his six years of selling at the festival.

“Everybody's great, and supporting one another, and eager to talk about how their experience has been, look at each other's work, and purchase each other’s work,” Ramos said. “Especially in this little stretch here, there's, like, four or five local artists who like come back multiple years. So it's always a good opportunity to catch up with people I haven't seen in a while.”

title: Increased AI usage leaves English professors pondering its role in writing
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Large language models capable of generating text from data have been on the rise, and discourse on how to handle artificial intelligence (AI) use in writing submissions has risen alongside it.

Using AI in the writing process isn’t a new development: Spellcheckers like Grammarly use AI, and free AI models are widely accessible online. Penn State even rolled out its AI Studio tool recently, giving students free access to paid models of ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini.

Many professors, including Julia Kasdorf, the director of Penn State’s undergraduate creative writing program, are now working to account for submissions written using AI.

Kasdorf, an English professor, oversees the school’s writing contests along with the program’s assistant director, Ellen Skirvin. She said the risk of awarding an AI-assisted or generated submission increases as entries continue to be accepted for the 2026 to 2027 year.

But she added that this risk shouldn’t be a reason to give up on highlighting work from creative writing classes and sources, like Kalliope, the university’s undergraduate literary magazine.

“I consider using AI to win a creative writing contest cheating,” Kasdorf said. “I would not want to win a prize that way.”

In May, the literary magazine Granta published the short story “The Serpent in the Grove” after its author, Jamir Nazir, won the Commonwealth Foundation’s prize for a short story in the Caribbean region.

However, Nazir’s story has been accused of having “obvious markers” — multiple “not x, but y” statements and lists of three — according to The Guardian. The Guardian also reported that “The Serpent in the Grove” had been entered into AI detection platforms that returned verdicts suggesting AI was potentially involved in writing the short story.

It being awarded despite the accusations highlights to Kasdorf how even experienced readers may not be able to detect the use of AI.

“Can we know for sure that a text wasn’t entirely written by AI? Some faculty members believe they can tell, and they would not select that piece of writing for an award if they suspected the use of AI,” Kasdorf said.

Wesley Boozer, an associate teaching professor of English, does not forbid his students from using AI to assist with brainstorming, outlining and proofreading their submissions, but forbids them from using it to assist in writing their drafts.

Boozer said this distinction is important to ensure students develop their own writing style, without AI being a “crutch to get them over the finish line.”

“As a writing teacher, I need students to write their text, but they can use AI to support their writing, and that's fine with me,” Boozer said. “In a classroom, we can have productive discussions about what students are discovering is the best practice of AI and how it can help — how they can use it efficiently and also ethically.”

He said writing assignments should also be revised to minimize the possibility of using AI for them. Local and personal subjects, Boozer said, might prevent students from submitting AI writing, because there won’t be as much data online for a model to pull from.

Julian Darragjati, an associate teaching professor of English, said he doesn’t want to see AI writing in anything he’s grading. But despite it crossing his desk more often than he said he’d like it to, he has chosen to keep his AI policies to a minimum.

Darragjati said he has considered new ways to combat his students using AI in their writing, including having students draft their work by hand while in class. He’s still uncertain when he will implement these policies.

“This is still an evolving technology,” Darragjati said. “It’s not easy to know how to deal with it.”

Regardless of what direction Darragjati chooses to take with his AI policies, he said it is critical that students continue to encounter the struggles that come with the writing process so they are better at deducing AI “slop from good writing.”

“There are no tricks, no shortcuts and improvement is slow. But wrestling with difficulties is necessary for learning, and AI gives you the illusion that you can write something worthwhile with ease,” Darragjati said. “If we’re giving feedback to AI, who is actually learning?”

title: Penn State baseball commit Alex Weingartner selected by Chicago White Sox in 2026 MLB Draft
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A potential future Nittany Lion has gone off the board.

Penn State commit Alex Weingartner was chosen in the sixth round by the Chicago White Sox in the 2026 MLB Draft.

Weingartner was listed as a position player when selected, but also has pitching potential that makes him a highly sought-after recruit and prospect.

The New Jersey native reaches the upper 90s on his fastball while also holding plus speed on the base paths.

Weingartner joins his brother, and former Penn State shortstop, Ryan Weingartner, as MLB draft picks. Alex Weingartner's selection marks the second player with Penn State connections joining designated hitter Michael Anderson as draftees.

It's unknown whether Alex Weingartner will honor his commitment to Penn State or forgo his college career and join the White Sox organization. If he plays for the Nittany Lions, he could be reselected in the MLB draft in 2029.

title: Penn State baseball's Michael Anderson selected by Texas Rangers in 2026 MLB Draft
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Mike Gambino has sent another player to the big leagues.

Penn State pitcher and designated hitter Michael Anderson was selected by the Texas Rangers in the fifth round of the 2026 MLB Draft.

While Anderson only played at Penn State for one season, he led the Nittany Lions at the plate all year. Anderson previously played for Rhode Island and Arkansas before throwing on blue and white threads — totaling 103 career games and 99 starts as an infielder at those schools.

Throughout his season at Penn State, Anderson totaled 53 RBI’s, 68 hits, and 11 doubles in 50 total games. He also made two appearances on the mound before injuring his arm, throwing 1.2 innings and striking out a batter.

Anderson’s offensive efforts this season earned him first-team All-Big Ten honors, ranking him second for home runs in the Big Ten Conference, with 20 total.

The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native will join former Penn State center fielder Paxton Kling in the Rangers' system.

Anderson continuously dominates as a right-handed hitter, excelling at consistent hit production. He was selected as a two-way player, giving him the opportunity to hit and pitch in the minor leagues.

description:
A former Penn State player is joining his fourth team in his collegiate career.

Forward Saša Ciani has transferred to Sacramento State, the team announced Friday.

Ciani spent last season with the Nittany Lions, playing in 28 games. He began the year with four-straight starts but became a rotational player in the frontcourt after freshman Ivan Jurić took over the starting center duties.

title: Off the record | Mind the gap
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear reader,

Every relationship, platonic, romantic or otherwise, is a direct reflection of the standards we hold for ourselves and how we are treated. Even though it’s apparent in friendships, it needs to be at the forefront of our minds when picking a partner. There’s absolutely no reason for you to settle for someone who doesn’t have a basic level of respect for you.

There’s a plethora of things that I could say inspired this, but a couple of months ago, I was walking in downtown State College, and I watched a girl fall off the curb in her high heels. The guy (I assume her date) stood there. He then looked down and kept walking. Yes, he just kept walking.

It’s actually quite repulsive, and I can only hope she’s no longer with him. But let this serve as a learning experience for us all. Your standards while dating need to be astronomically high. Not, “Oh, he held the door open for me one time,” but expecting the very best from the person you spend your time with.

Maybe this is hitting a little too close to home, and if it is, I need you to go ahead and do the thing we’re both thinking.

This doesn’t make you a snob, or picky. You’re allowed to want the best of the best. I know people who let their partners get away with the bare minimum, who let their partners get praised for things I would do for my friends with zero hesitation.

No, a gap isn’t taking a break from the person themselves, but rather noticing the gap between a quality that you possess and one they don’t. Most of my favorite people are in relationships where there’s a humor gap. She’s the funniest person I’ve ever met, and he’s a bore.

I wish this were a concept I, myself, magically created. Instead, I owe credit to the girls on TikTok who recognize there are no gaps in their relationships. They’ll post something with their partner that highlights the lack of a “humor gap,” “intelligence gap,” or “political gap.” This is the perfect phrasing, because if these characteristics are of value to you, the gap should be nonexistent.

Gaps and standards go hand in hand. If you aren’t settling within your relationship, then you won’t be encountering gaps. And if you find yourself with a gap in a part of your life that’s important to you, ditch the person widening it and find someone who fills it.

I’m not saying all the aforementioned gaps are going to be equally important to you. Maybe you don’t care if your partner is funny, or if they’re of your same political affiliation.

If you do care, finding someone who isn’t your complete opposite may be your saving grace. I think “opposites attract” is only somewhat true. There’s no way it’s going to work if you are completely different people, with different morals, interests and beliefs.

It should, however, be a deal breaker to come to a point in your relationship where you sacrifice your dignity. True character cannot be replaced by the facade of charisma, and if you have self-respect, the former should carry more weight.

And yes, I hear how this all sounds. All my advice ends with the same thing: Break up. I’m guilty of throwing around the phrase at any inconvenience, whether it’s a friend complaining over coffee about their situationship or a friend crying on my couch about their partner.

We’re all way too comfortable staying in relationships where compatibility is nonexistent. Forcing an unfulfilling relationship only burdens you in the long run.

These gaps are warning signs that it’s not going to work, and we need to be paying attention. The longer you continue to settle for someone who you know in your heart isn’t right for you, the further you dig your heels into a place you aren’t meant to be.

From experience, your self-worth needs to be stronger than your feelings for them. You need to be able to recognize that gaps of kindness, humor and drive outweigh the other things they’re showing you.

The gaps may even present themselves after the two of you are done, and that’s the greatest gift of all — showing you what you no longer have to deal with.

Here’s to hoping you reflect on the gaps in your life and act accordingly.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

title: Fire departments respond to fire at Alpha Zeta fraternity house
author: Mia Debelevich | Managing Editor
description:
Editor’s Note: This story was updated Thursday evening.

Firefighters responded to a fire at the Alpha Zeta fraternity house on West Campus Thursday afternoon after smoke was reported coming from its roof, according to Centre Region Fire Director Shawn Kauffman.

Kauffman said contractors had been working on the house when the fire broke out, and no residents were in the property. There are no reported injuries.

The cause of the fire is still unknown, according to Kauffman, but is most likely related to the construction activity. It has since been put out.

He said there is heavy damage in the attic, and the rest of the building sustained heavy water and smoke damage. The property will be uninhabitable for "a period of time."

Pleasant Gap, Undine, Boalsburg and Port Matilda fire departments were on scene alongside State College’s Alpha Fire Company.

At 4:12 p.m., Penn State sent an alert urging people to avoid the area on North Burrowes Road between Curtin Street and Pollock Road.

title: Top athletes at each Penn State Commonwealth campus for 2026
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Sports are a key part of student life at Penn State, but University Park is just one of many campuses.

The achievements of those at the blue and white’s satellite campuses often fly under the radar, but the Penn State brand still makes noise beyond State College, stretching to every corner of the Commonwealth.

Here are the standouts from nine branch campuses, and runner-up honorable mentions.

Abington: Donato Incollingo, baseball

For a blue and white unit that finished an impressive 32-13 and went deep into the United East Tournament, Incollingo was the engine. The Huntingdon Valley, Pennsylvania, native slashed .399/.503/.842 with a team-high 17 home runs.

As for accolades, Incollingo was the 2026 United East Conference Player of the Year, an All-Region IV first team honoree by the America Baseball Coaches Association and D3baseball.com.

HM: Amirah Hackey, women’s basketball: 17.6 PPG, 5.8 RPG, 2 APG and 2.7 BPG – Lady Lions finished 20-7, United East Tournament runner-ups

Altoona: Riley Best, softball

After two seasons with Lock Haven, Best had a career year in her first season with a blue and white jersey, helping the Nittany Lions reach the Allegheny Mountain Collegiate Conference Championship Game, which they dropped to Penn State Behrend.

The Team MVP was named to the National Fastpitch Coaches Association Division III All-Region VII second-team, as well as the AMCC Player of the Year. Best led the conference in hits, home runs, RBI, slugging percentage and total bases, while finishing second in batting average.

HM: Lia Simanski, women’s volleyball: 267 kills, 22 service aces – Penn State finished 14-14, Simanski earned first team All-AMCC honors, was named Team MVP and campus Female Scholar-Athlete of the Year.

Beaver: Bailey Reig, women’s basketball

Penn State Beaver women’s basketball had an amazing season, finishing 23-6 and going deep into the postseason. The Lady Lions lost to Penn State York in the PSUAC Tournament and went on to reach the USCAA Division II National Championship.

In the blue and white’s Consolation Game victory in the bracket over Kent State-Tuscarawas, Reig scored a game-high 25 points. All season, the sophomore starred from the shooting guard position, averaging 15.2 points per game en route to earning PSUAC All-Conference honors as a member of the West First Team.

HM: Bryanna Neiberg, women’s volleyball: 240 kills, 59 SA – Penn State finished 9-17, Neiberg was named the only other USCAA All-American first team mention for Beaver outside of Reig and earned a PSUAC All-Conference second team nod.

Behrend: Dylan Barnes, baseball

The 2026 Team MVP put up all the statistics to back up the award this season, posting a .417 batting average along with a team-high seven long shots.

The Nittany Lions finished 31-12, and won the AMCC Championship Game in which Barnes had an RBI base knock. Penn State Behrend also appeared in the NCAA Division III Championship Regionals but failed to win a game. Barnes was named AMCC Player of the Year, an All-AMCC First Team selection and was named to the 2026 D3baseball.com All-Region 7 Team.

HM: Julia Jones (SB): Over 41 games played, posted team high.421 BA, also appeared on the mound, primarily as a reliever. Penn State finished 28-13 and was crowned AMCC Tournament Champions.

Berks: Talan Nguyen, men’s tennis

Penn State Behrend had a relatively successful season, going 7-6 and making it to the semifinals of the United East Championship Tournament before falling to Penn State Harrisburg.

The No. 1 singles player for the Nittany Lions all season was Nguyen, who was named to the UEC first-team as a singles player and the second-team with his doubles partner, Jazae Maina-Itegi.

HM: Maura Gorman, softball: - .299 BA, 10 RBI, .402 OBP (team best), 3.61 ERA, 54 1/3 IP, and 29 K. Penn State finished 10-21, lost to Wilson College in the UEC Tournament.

Brandywine: April Lewandowski, softball

As the lone UEC first team selection for the Brandywine softball team, she anchored a pitching staff that complemented a lineup that earned the campus's first-ever national personal awards.

Despite that batting stardom, Lewandowski was the most crucial to the blue and white’s successes. With a team-high 123 2/3 innings pitched, she was a workhorse in the circle, posting a 2.38 ERA with 116 strikeouts in the process. The highlight of Lewandowski’s season came with a no-hitter and a perfect game, both against Cedar Crest, once in the regular season and once in the postseason.

HM: Jayden Fogg, (MBB): Earned campus Male Athlete of the Year and second team All-United East honors, en route to Penn State finishing 17-10 and claiming a share of the regular season title. Averaged 11.5 PPG and 5.7 RPG.

DuBois: Natalie Bowser, women’s basketball & softball

Bowser was a two-sport athlete for DuBois, yet unlike some, she thrived at both this season. Primarily, her success came on the court, where she averaged a double-double, with 2.44 blocks per game, which earned her USCAA All-American First Team honors and PSUAC West Player of the Year.

On the diamond, Bowser posted a .354 batting average, a team high nine home runs and a tied team best 28 RBI.

HM: Isaac Tiracorda (BB): Penn State finished 32-8, with a USCAA Small College World Series appearance. The sophomore slashed .430/.551.729 with seven home runs and 38 RBI in 37 games. Named USCAA SCWS Postseason first team All-American.

Fayette: Kylie Stevenson, softball

Penn State may have only finished 8-18-1, yet despite the bumps, the Nittany Lions found their form in the postseason behind Stevenson.

The blue and white captured its first-ever PSUAC Tournament Championship, where Stevenson smashed three hits and two RBI. The Nittany Lions earned a berth in the USCAA Tournament, but couldn’t advance. Stevenson posted a .571 batting average with 27 RBI on the season.

HM: Jesse Lawson Jr., men’s basketball: Only 10 GP, 19.7 PPG, 3.7 RPG and 1.9APG, was named to PSUAC West Division First Team.

Greater Allegheny: Eric Valentin, baseball

In a successful season on the diamond, Valentin led the campaign from the batter’s box. The junior posted a team high nine home runs, 39 RBI and .420 batting average. Valentin racked in the personal accolades behind that strong year at the plate, earning USCAA First Team All-American honors and being named the PSUAC Baseball West Player of the Year.

HM: Linda Shepard, softball: Posted a .440 BA, 39 RBI, 9HR, 2.68 ERA, 86 1/3 IP and 51K. Named PSUAC Softball Player of the Year.

title: Potential 2026 Penn State defensive breakout candidates
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State will have many new faces on the defensive side of the ball, but it’s still littered with veteran players, leaving breakout candidates limited for the 2026 season.

Regardless, there are some budding young stars and other players primed to break out. Here’s a look at some of those candidates in Matt Campbell’s first year at the helm.

Daryus Dixson

Some could argue that Daryus Dixson already had his breakout at the end of his freshman year, but he has the chance to take an even bigger step in his sophomore year.

Dixson is expected to be a full-time starter at cornerback in a very experienced room, and with Audavion Collins and Zion Tracy around him, he has the chance to take that next step and be the lockdown cornerback the Nittany Lions have been looking for.

The “breakout” Dixson could have is more so him playing full time this year and showcasing more of the consistent cornerback that was seen in the final stretch of 2025.

Zion Tracy

Tracy is a consensus starter likely at the nickel position for Penn State. However, after his first full season as a starter, Tracy’s ceiling seems to be unlimited.

After missing time at the beginning of the year due to an injury, Tracy started nine games last season, notching 32 total tackles and two pass breakups. Tracy’s mix of speed and talent could put him in position to have a dominant 2026.

“I think Zion Tracy is one of the best football players that maybe I have coached, to be quite honest with you, in terms of talent, ability,” Campbell said in March.

Campbell’s view on Tracy showcases that he still has untapped potential that he could unlock during his senior year and become a difference maker for the defense.

Max Granville

Max Granville has already been with Penn State for two years, but he hasn’t seen any substantial playing time yet due to a season-ending injury last year. Regardless, the redshirt sophomore has always been spoken of highly.

This season, Granville could step into one of the two starting defensive end spots and be a key contributor in a role that many current NFL pass rushers have succeeded in.

Granville’s attention to detail and film study on former Nittany Lions could add him to the growing list of Penn State pass rushers in the NFL like Abdul Carter, Micah Parsons and Chop Robinson, all of which he’s studied to help his game grow.

Yvan Kemajou

The pass rusher on the opposite side of Granville is likely to be Yvan Kemajou. The sophomore showed flashes of being a strong pass rusher but never garnered enough playing time to fully showcase his potential.

Kemajou began to play more and make a larger impact during the end of the year. In the final two regular season games against Nebraska and Rutgers, Kemajou notched 1.5 sacks as Terry Smith let younger players get more burn.

Now in a position to be one of the main pass rushers, Kemajou could take a major jump and grow from his freshman season to add to the lineage of edge rushers that have come through Penn State.

Armstrong Nnodim

The defensive tackle room has a lot of experience, but Armstrong Nnodim could work himself into a starting role as a younger player.

Nnodim made his way to Happy Valley after two years at Oklahoma State where he made some impact in his redshirt freshman season. The defensive tackle totaled 11 tackles and two tackles for loss in 12 games as a rotational lineman.

The redshirt sophomore has already made a jump in practice against experienced offensive linemen, and his size will be a different look than how previous Penn State defensive linemen have been built.

“You could say he's an absolute bruiser,” offensive lineman Cooper Cousins said in April. “He's hard to move. He's 315 pounds, he's got like 18% body fat. He's a freak.”

title: Strength coach Reid Kagy talks player success at Lift for Life
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State players gathered inside Holuba Hall last week for the 23rd annual Lift for Life to raise money for Uplifting Athletes, a nonprofit.

After the players raised more than $40,000 for youth battling rare diseases, Reid Kagy, the director of strength and conditioning, spoke with reporters in State College about the event and summer workouts over the last several weeks.

Kagy also provided updates on a few key players recovering from injury. Here’s a look at what he had to say about these specific guys last Wednesday.

Tony Rojas’ recovery

Tony Rojas missed most of the 2025 season after tearing his ACL during practice ahead of Penn State’s trip to UCLA. His recovery stretched through the spring and summer, limiting his participation at spring practice.

As summer workouts have ramped up over the last few weeks, Rojas has gradually returned to the field.

“Tony’s progressing and (continuing) to do a great job,” Kagy said. “I’m proud of Tony.”

During the offseason, Rojas’ impact extended beyond the field. As the new coaching staff introduced changes to the program, he became a steady presence for returning players despite working through his own recovery.

“Everybody was experiencing change in the program, so I appreciate Tony for the leadership that he displayed,” Kagy said. “I appreciate Tony for doing all those things that it takes to create a winning football team.”

Even though he wasn’t able to participate in spring workouts, Kagy said Rojas continued to influence the program through his preparation and mentorship, and the linebacker remained actively involved in the team’s development during the spring.

“He was creating relationships, he’s helping other linebackers get through the defense, he’s learning in the meeting rooms,” Kagy said. “The same thing for him is we had an opportunity to take a step and grow as a team during the spring, and Tony was a large part of that. That’s what’s great about Tony.”

Armstrong Nnodim’s relationship building

Defensive tackle Armstrong Nnodim has made himself known among the Nittany Lions after transferring in from Oklahoma State both on and off the field.

“It’s usually somebody’s birthday every single day, Armstrong finds out,” Kagy said. “Whether it’s a team meeting or a team setting in any capacity, Armstrong (sings happy birthday).”

On the field and in the weight room, Armstrong has wowed the coaches with his work ethic and discipline, and he’s on track to play a large role on Penn State’s defensive line this year.

“He’s extremely talented, he’s extremely physically talented, he makes me look good,” Kagy said. “What’s been impressive is his work ethic.”

Max Granville adds depth

In addition to Nnodim, redshirt sophomore Max Granville is another player working toward a big role on the defensive line this season.

While Granville is recovering from a season-ending injury he picked up working out at home ahead of last season along with a setback later on, he’s impressed the coaches through as he’s worked his way back into practice.

“Max is somebody we were super excited about when we stepped in here,” Kagy said. “I’ve been super impressed by his work ethic. We’re really excited about what Max can do.”

Expectations were high for Granville before his injury after he reclassified and joined Penn State ahead of the 2024 season, during which he appeared in seven games and showed promise.

Now, Granville has his chance to breakout as a redshirt sophomore.

“The biggest thing about Max, Max is super humble and Max goes to work,” Kagy said. “(He’s) another guy that we've just been super impressed with from a work ethic standpoint and a guy that continues to impact this team.”

Rocco Becht’s development

After missing a majority of the spring season to rehab after shoulder surgery, Rocco Becht has taken a step forward in his return.

While he has yet to practice at full strength, Kagy and other coaches have been pushing Becht and helping him recover and improve.

“Every single day, we’re trying to make sure we build up some of that resiliency so he can play healthy,” Kagy said. “He’s getting stronger.”

Becht started three seasons for Iowa State, taking on the role of team leader. Although he arrived in Happy Valley only months ago, Kagy said Becht has already earned the respect of his teammates at Penn State.

“Rocco’s a leader and Rocco is a team guy,” Kagy said. “His toughness bleeds into the rest of his team. Even coming back from injury, he made an impact in any way he could.”

With his shoulder still recovering from surgery, Becht has been limited on the number of throwing reps he can take during practice. Instead, he’s worked on his lower body strength and scrambling ability.

“Roc is getting faster,” Kagy said. “Is he gonna run a route? No, but he’s gonna run, and he loves to talk about how he’s gonna run the ball.”

title: Film review | 2028 QB James Armstrong brings true dual-threat play style to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State secured its third commit in the Class of 2028 when 4-star quarterback James Armstrong pledged on Thursday.

The in-state gunslinger ranks as the No. 10 quarterback nationally and No. 4 player out of Pennsylvania from 2028, per 247Sports composite. He’s the first commit in the class on the offensive side of the ball.

Armstrong had a solid sophomore season at Hopewell High School, racking up 2,232 passing yards for 21 touchdowns and three interceptions plus 799 rushing yards and 16 touchdowns on the ground.

Here’s a deeper look into his film.

A true dual threat

When the pocket collapses, Armstrong shows no hesitation in calling his own number. Whether a lane appears in the interior or outside, he’ll recognize the opening early and make a break for it.

Armstrong possesses blinding speed and the balance to stay on his feet even when outnumbered. He executes swift cuts and juke maneuvers, weaving between the defenders while shedding tackles.

In addition, he isn’t afraid to lower his shoulder and truck the secondary when there’s no way forward. That competitive edge is put on full display in the red zone, as Armstrong doesn’t shy away from diving forward for a gritty touchdown.

When given the time and space to make a throw, Armstrong showcases his talent for hitting receivers in stride up the middle and down the sidelines. His wind-up is quick, allowing him to get the pass off as soon as possible.

Armstrong’s solid when throwing on the run as well. His first instinct is to tuck the ball and take it himself, but he’ll look for a quick pass upfield when he knows that rushing the ball isn’t optimal. His accuracy remains intact regardless of whether he sets his feet before throwing.

Quick release, quick decisions

Armstrong’s game is all about efficiency. His throws can come in a fraction of a second, so he avoids getting sacked when his offensive line fails to keep rushers at bay.

Additionally, he appears comfortable slinging the ball from all different arm slots depending on the situation at-hand.

When being blitzed, his eyes move fast, and he’s able to locate the open target directly in front of him, making short to mid-range throws before the defense closes in. If Armstrong gets any more time, he’ll look for a deep ball downfield.

Over the shoulder dimes

While the short pass is his go-to when things get dicey, Armstrong’s over-the-shoulder lob shouldn’t be taken lightly.

Part of what makes Armstrong so effective is how he leads the receiver with his passes. It doesn’t have to be a slant route up the middle or corner route toward the back of the end zone, as Armstrong will meet the receiver in stride anywhere to keep their momentum going.

Armstrong does this best on straight routes along the sidelines. He executes over-the-shoulder passes well, hitting his receiver right in front of their inside shoulder while also limiting the chances of being intercepted.

Should the receiver get the separation he needs to make the catch without the defender interjecting, he’s either getting pushed out of bounds or taking it to the house thanks to Armstrong’s ball placement.
