title: Borough Council passes ordinance barring borough collaboration with ICE
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
The State College Borough Council voted unanimously to pass an ordinance including provisions that prevent borough resources and personnel, including State College police officers, from involvement with Immigrations and Customs Enforcement (ICE) operations on Monday.

Protestors gathered outside of the State College Municipal Building, where the meeting was being held, in opposition of the ordinance, codified as ordinance no. 2247.

Its passing prevents State College from enrolling in any partnerships under Section 287 of the Immigration and Nationality Act or “any successor program” that delegates federal immigration enforcement authority to local authorities.

The council also passed a resolution stating the borough’s principles on immigration enforcement regarding public safety and constitutional rights.

Other agreements for the sole purpose of civil immigration enforcement are barred. Borough personnel cannot collect someone’s immigration status — save for “determining eligibility for a specific service, benefit, or public employment as required by federal or state law or is reasonably necessary in the course of a criminal investigation for a violation of state or local law, or court order.”

John Hayes was the only council member to vote against the resolution.

Hayes said he saw the resolution’s purpose as being a "messaging bill” meant to advance a specific political position without seriously changing public policy. He said its text explicitly stated its contents were “aspirational and advisory only.”

However, Council President Evan Myers said the resolution was created because the borough cannot include specific things in the ordinance, like banning facemasks on ICE agents, as they’re not legally enforceable by the borough.

While the resolution can't be included as an additional provision to the ordinance, Myers said it’s important to have a document expressing the borough’s beliefs on ICE operations in local communities.

“There are certain things that we expect based on our home rule charter. Things that we expect as human beings, the way other people should act. And even though we can't codify them — because we don't have that ability to do that — we can at least state them again as a corollary to what we just passed,” Myers said.

Sylus Smith, a member of Centre County Democratic Socialists of America, said he understands the concerns of the resolution ending up as a symbolic gesture, but it would still have an impact on those living and working in the borough.

“It reassures members of the public, such as myself, such as my neighbors and particularly my many classmates at Penn State who are perhaps not so familiar with which ordinances have been passed when, that this council does believe in the human rights of immigrants,” Smith, a fourth-year studying political science, said.

Smith said the resolution and the ordinance were examples of how a local government worked to meet the demands of its constituents. He added that it shows the borough’s continued support for the communities it serves, making it “a little more than purely worthless symbolism.”

“Too often, we have politicians who seem to believe that their responsibilities begin and end with speeches and a vote,” Smith said. “Too often, they forget that as members of this community with such a high standing and with such connections to various organizations, that they can be part of bringing us all together and organizing us so that we can make more meaningful actions against injustices like this one.”

title: Penn State RB commit Aiden Gibson flips to Rutgers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Penn State commit has found a new home.

Four-star running back Aiden Gibson announced his flip to Rutgers on Tuesday.

Gibson, the No. 10 running back nationally, per 247Sports composite, committed to Penn State on May 12. Two days later, 3-star Chukwuma Odoh pledged, but now Odoh is the lone running back commit for Penn State in the Class of 2027.

The South Carolina native is also set to reclassify to the 2026 class, enrolling at Rutgers in August.

Following the announcement, Gibson thanked Penn State on X, saying, "Thank you Penn State for the amazing opportunity yall have given me and treated me."

The Nittany Lions are down to just 21 commits now in the 2027 class following flips from Gibson, wide receiver Jamir Dean and cornerbacks Semajay Robinson and Zachary Gleason Jr. over the past two months.

title: Former Penn State WR commit Khalil Taylor pledges to Nebraska
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State commit is headed to a Big Ten foe.

Wide receiver Khalil Taylor has committed back to Nebraska, he announced via the Rivals' YouTube channel on Monday.

Taylor ranks as the No. 84 player in the 2027 class, the No. 13 wide receiver and No. 4 player in Pennsylvania.

Taylor initially committed to Penn State on March 29, 2025 under James Franklin but decommitted on Oct. 10, a day before the Nittany Lions’ loss to UCLA.

The Gibsonia, Pennsylvania, native has consistently visited Happy Valley since the hiring of Matt Campbell, including a junior day visit on Jan. 24.

Taylor plays at WPIAL powerhouse Pine Richland, which made the WPIAL Championship in 2025.

The 5-foot-11 wide receiver returned to Penn State for an official visit on the weekend of June 5, but the Nittany Lions couldn't beat out Nebraska to land a top in-state prospect.
